title: Spaun Surges at Texas Open: Masters Week Momentum with One Hand to Play
excerpt: J.J. Spaun rallied to win the Valero Texas Open on Sunday, claiming his third PGA Tour title just days before the Masters. Robert MacIntyre missed out on a second title of 2026.
body:
One Last Warm-Up Before Augusta

J.J. Spaun seized the momentum heading into Masters week by winning the Valero Texas Open at TPC San Antonio on Sunday, shooting a closing 67 to finish 17-under par for a one-shot victory. The win marks Spaun's third PGA Tour title of his career and his second at this San Antonio venue, where he claimed his maiden tour title in 2022. Scotland's Robert MacIntyre, who led by one shot going into the final round, was unable to force a play-off, settling for a share of second place alongside England's Matt Wallace and America's Michael Kim at 16 under.

Winning Score
-17
Spaun's Tour Titles
3
Final Margin
One Shot
Runners-Up
3-Way Tie

MacIntyre's Masters Chase Stumbles

The 29-year-old Scot arrived at the Texas Open nursing a powerful run of form. He led by four shots after two rounds and looked primed for a second title in 2026. However, torrential storms curtailed Saturday's play dramatically, forcing MacIntyre to absorb a heavy Sunday schedule of 30 holes. He finished his third round with a frustrating bogey on 18 and went into the final 18 with a two-shot advantage. But Spaun had other ideas. The American sprinted clear with three birdies on the back nine, including an eagle at the driveable par-four 17th — precisely where Spaun would ultimately seal the title.

Spaun's Reset

"This game is so crazy," Spaun said afterwards. "I've not been at the form I wanted based off last season. It means a lot to come back and win here at a place that's been so good to me." The 35-year-old had endured a difficult stretch after his 2024 U.S. Open triumph, putting immense pressure on himself to repeat. But Spaun found his way back to the mantra that carried him through his breakthrough campaign: putting less pressure on himself. The results speak for themselves. That mentality shift could be crucial in the days ahead.

Masters Awaits

There is precious little time for celebration or regret. The Masters begins on Thursday at Augusta National, and both Spaun and MacIntyre will arrive this week with fresh momentum — one with a trophy in hand, the other with a clear idea of where his game stands. Scottie Scheffler remains the betting favourite at 5/1, but the Texas Open proved once again that Augusta's unique demands open the door for surprise contenders. Spaun, riding back-to-back adrenaline, could easily exploit those possibilities.
